narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 76.5% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile shows strong alignment with traits of students who struggled, including bottom-decile rankings in both your first- and third-year law school grades, a low LSAT score of 25, and an undergraduate GPA of 2.7—all well below typical students' averages of around the middle of their classes, 37 on the LSAT, and 3.2 GPA. Coming from a middle-income family, being a white female planning full-time studies, you match the majority of applicants in demographics, which didn't sway the outcome much. The model's decision hinged most heavily on your third-year class decile, as being in the lowest group strongly signaled higher bar failure risk compared to peers in higher deciles. Your LSAT score was the next biggest factor pushing toward that prediction, far lower than what successful bar takers usually score. Similarly, your first-year decile played a key role, reinforcing concerns about early academic struggles that often persist. Your family income level and undergraduate GPA followed closely, both contributing to the failure likelihood since they're below what's common for passers. Your racial background had a modest influence in the same direction, while your full-time status barely factored in. Interestingly, your gender as female provided a tiny nudge toward predicting success, unlike the other elements. Overall, these academic markers dominated the decision, far outweighing demographics. To boost your future bar exam odds, focus on retaking the LSAT for a higher score if possible, as it's one factor you might improve with preparation. Strengthening study habits or seeking tutoring could help replicate better grade deciles in any future program, addressing the core academic predictors head-on. With targeted effort, you can shift your trajectory away from this prediction.
